# Reactive Robotics 2.0 — Course Overview
## Welcome

<!-- type: lesson -->

Welcome

![Screen Shot 2023-07-12 at 12.32.13 PM.png](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/curriculum_editing/course_banner.png)

Hello and welcome to **Robotics Engineer I: Reactive Robots 2.0 !**

In this **intermediate-level LEGO SPIKE Prime course**, you'll take your robotics skills to the next level by designing and programming robots that **react** to their surroundings. Building on fundamental concepts, you’ll learn how to integrate sensors, create interactive behaviors, and develop more advanced movement strategies.

By the end of this course, you’ll not only refine your programming and building skills but also gain the confidence to create robots that think and respond dynamically to the world around them.

Let’s get started on this exciting journey into **Reactive Robots**

**  Course Learning Outcomes**

By the end of the **Reactive Robots 2.0** course, students will be able to:


- 
**Control Structures:** Utilize control blocks and loops to create efficient and structured robot behaviors.

- 
**Parallel Programming:** Implement concurrent execution of tasks, allowing the robot to handle multiple actions simultaneously.

- 
**Functions & Variables:** Organize and store data effectively to improve program efficiency and modularity.

- 
**Force Sensor:** Use force sensor inputs to trigger actions, enabling responsive and interactive robot designs.

- 
**Beginning Passive Attachments:** Design and implement basic mechanical components such as bumpers, wedges, plows, hooks, and forks for physical interactions.

- 
**Intermediate Passive Attachments:** Integrate more advanced mechanisms like one-way doors and carabiners to enhance controlled interactions.

- 
**Ultrasonic Sensor:** Program the ultrasonic sensor for distance detection, enabling navigation and obstacle avoidance.


Through hands-on challenges and guided problem-solving, students will refine their **robotic engineering** and **programming** skills, preparing them for more complex robotics projects.

# Class 0: Release Notes
## Class 0: Notes

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 0: Notes

### **Coach’s Notes – Reactive Robots 2.0**

At this stage, students should have **strong mastery of basic movement**. This means that **"close enough" is not acceptable**—we are now expecting precise execution of turns and movement. Students must **fully complete each task as instructed**, reinforcing accuracy and control in their programming.

### **Coaching Tips & Encouragement**


1. 

**Plan Before Executing**


  - Students should **strategize** before diving into challenges.

  - Encourage the use of **pseudocode and mission pathing** to break down the task before coding.

- Encourage the use of **pseudocode and mission pathing** to break down the task before coding.

  - A clear plan leads to more efficient problem-solving.



2. 

**Use Comments & Sensory Feedback**


  - 
**Commenting code** helps with understanding and debugging.

  - 
**Auditory (beeps) and visual (LED changes) cues** can make troubleshooting easier.



3. 

**Design Attachments with Flexibility**


  - Remind students that **robots are rarely perfect**—attachments should allow for slight variations in positioning.

  - A well-designed attachment can **compensate for small errors** in movement.



4. 

**Troubleshoot Step by Step**


  - Instead of guessing what’s wrong, students should **break the code into chunks** and test piece by piece.

- Instead of guessing what’s wrong, students should **break the code into chunks** and test piece by piece.

  - Encourage them to **start at the beginning and add lines gradually** to pinpoint failures.

  - This structured approach saves time compared to trial-and-error guessing.



5. 

**Meet All Challenge Requirements**


  - Ensure students are **following every rule as written**—no skipping steps!

  - If their solution doesn’t fully meet the criteria, they **need to refine and correct it** before moving on.




By reinforcing these habits, students will **develop stronger problem-solving skills, cleaner code, and more reliable robots**.

## Class 2:  Loops

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 2:  Loops

![robot](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/robothands.png)

In this class, we’re diving into an awesome programming concept called **loops**! Loops help your code **repeat actions automatically**, making your robots smarter and more efficient.


Imagine you want your robot to drive forward until it “sees” a wall or keep dancing until a button is pressed—loops make that happen! They’re the secret sauce that makes your robot’s code super smart and flexible, ready to tackle any adventure you throw at it!

Let’s jump into the exciting world of SPIKE Prime control blocks! We’ll uncover what each one can do in the future lessons. Below is a photo of all the control blocks available for us to use. 



![control blocks](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/cb1.png)

![control blocks](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/cb2.png)

## Class 2: Repeat Loop Block

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 2: Repeat Loop Block

![jump](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R600V2/jump.png)

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 2: Repeat Loop Block

![jump](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R600V2/jump.png)

The *repeat* command in coding is like a shortcut that tells the computer to perform a specific action multiple times without needing to write each action individually. Imagine you want your character to jump 10 times in a row. Instead of typing the "jump" command 10 separate times, you can use a repeat command to tell the computer, "repeat the jump 10 times."

Here’s how it works: the repeat command creates a loop, where everything inside it will run once, then loop back to the beginning and run again until the specified number of times is reached. It’s efficient and keeps your code clean and short, especially when you need something done over and over!

Below is an example of how to make your robot drive in a square. 


![jump](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/squarecode.png)

Here's an example below of how to make a robot drive in a square—first without a loop, then with a loop. Notice how the version with a loop is much cleaner and more efficient! 

![code](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/sqw.png)

Without a loop, you have to repeat the same movement commands multiple times, making your code longer and harder to change later. With a loop, you can write the instructions once and let the robot repeat them, making your code more compact, easier to read, and simpler to modify if you want to adjust the square’s size or shape.

## Class 2: Forever Loop Block

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 2: Forever Loop Block

![roller coaster](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/rollercoaster.png)

Class 2: Forever Loop Block

![roller coaster](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/rollercoaster.png)

Alright, let’s think about a forever loop in Lego Spike Prime like this:

Imagine you’re at an amusement park, and you find your favorite ride—the one you want to go on again and again! Instead of waiting in line every time, you get a special "forever" pass that lets you stay on the ride as long as you want. As soon as the ride finishes, it just starts again, no waiting necessary! That’s how a forever loop works in programming with Lego Spike Prime.

In a forever loop, the robot keeps doing the same thing—like spinning in a circle, flashing a light, or playing a sound—over and over again until you tell it to stop (or turn it off). It’s a super useful way to make your robot do something non-stop, just like you would if you had unlimited rides at an amusement park!

![jump](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/floop.png)

The code example above will have the robot endlessly dancing in squares, keeping it moving until it either tires and the battery calls it quits!

Check it out: The forever loop has a flat bottom, like it's saying, "Nope, nothing's getting added here!" That’s because this loop just keeps going and going, looping forever without ever stopping to let anything else run.

# Class 3: Parallel Programming
## Class 3: Parallel Programming

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 3: Parallel Programming

### **From Sequential to Parallel Programming**

Up until now, you’ve been using **sequential programming**—where your robot follows a **step-by-step** set of instructions, completing one action before moving on to the next. Think of it like checking off items on a to-do list: the robot finishes **one task at a time** before starting the next.

But in this lesson, we’re stepping up to **parallel programming**!

### **What is Parallel Programming?**

But in this lesson, we’re stepping up to **parallel programming**!

### **What is Parallel Programming?**

Parallel programming (aka broadcasting) lets your SPIKE Prime robot perform **multiple tasks at the same time**, just like how you can **walk and talk simultaneously**. Instead of waiting for one task to finish before starting another, your robot can handle multiple actions at once—making it **faster and more efficient**.

### **Example:**

🔹 Imagine your robot needs to **move forward** and **lift a claw**.
🔹 In **sequential programming**, the robot would move first, stop, and then lift the claw.
🔹 With **parallel programming (broadcasting)** the robot can move and lift the claw **at the same time**, completing the task more efficiently!

In SPIKE Prime, this happens when you use blocks like **"When Program Starts"** or **"When I receive broadcasting message"** to tell the robot to **work on two or more tasks at once**. It’s like assigning **each action its own worker**, so they all operate together!

Parallel programming is also known as **concurrent programming** or **multithreading**—all of which mean your robot is handling **multiple actions simultaneously**.

Now, let’s put this into practice and make your robot even smarter and faster

## Class 3: Tips & Tricks - Wall Squaring

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 3: Tips & Tricks - Wall Squaring



**Example of Wall Squaring Code:**

To wall square you simple run into a wall for seconds. 

![code](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/wallsquarecode.png)

**Video Example of Wall Squaring:**

[Video: Programming Canvas](https://player.vimeo.com/video/1066429803)

 

**Reasons to Use Wall Squaring with a SPIKE Prime Robot:**


1. 


  1. 

**Fixes Drift** – The longer a robot drives, the more tiny errors add up, causing it to veer off course. Wall squaring resets its position for better accuracy.


  2. 

**Improves Mission Consistency** – By starting from a straight and known position, your robot is more likely to complete missions the same way every time.


  3.

3. 

**Compensates for Wheel Slip** – If one wheel slips even a little, the robot's heading changes. Wall squaring realigns both wheels to keep movement predictable.


  4. 

**Makes Attachments More Reliable** – If your robot isn’t properly aligned, attachments might miss their targets. Squaring up ensures they connect as intended.


  5. 

**Saves Time and Effort** – Instead of fine-tuning every movement in code, wall squaring provides a simple physical correction that improves accuracy without extra programming.


  6. 

**Easy to Implement** – Just drive into a sturdy surface, stop both motors, and let the wall do the work. It’s a quick and effective way to reset alignment mid-run!

# Class 4:  Variables
## Class 4: Variables Intro & Camel Case

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 4: Variables Intro & Camel Case

 

![moving boxes](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/vbox2.png)

Imagine you're managing a **big storage room** filled with important items—books, toys, and plates. To keep everything organized, you use **labeled boxes** to store each type of item separately. In **Scratch Word Blocks for SPIKE**, variables work just like these boxes! They **hold information** and can **change** whenever needed.

**CamelCase:**![camel](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/camel.png)

**CamelCase:**![camel](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/camel.png)

CamelCase is a way to name variables in coding that makes them **easy to read**. Imagine a camel 🐫—it has **humps**, and in CamelCase, words are joined together with **humps** (capital letters) to make them stand out!

Here are **simple rules** for using CamelCase when naming variables:

1️⃣ **No Spaces** – Join words together without spaces.


- ✅ `myVariable`


- ❌ `my variable`



2️⃣ **Start with a Lowercase Letter** – The first letter is always lowercase, but each new word starts with a capital letter.


- ✅ `robotSpeed`


- ❌ `RobotSpeed`



3️⃣ **Use Capital Letters for New Words** – This makes the name easy to read.


- ✅ `gameScore`


- ❌ `gamescore`

3️⃣ **Use Capital Letters for New Words** – This makes the name easy to read.


- ✅ `gameScore`


- ❌ `gamescore`



4️⃣ **Keep It Short but Clear** – Use names that describe what the variable does.


- ✅ `playerScore`


- ❌ `x`



5️⃣ **No Special Characters** – Use only letters and numbers (but don’t start with a number).

**Variables in the real world:**

![moving boxes](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/vgame.png)

### **How Do Video Games Use Variables?**

Think about your favorite video game. How does it **remember your score**? How does it **track your health, coins, or level progress**?

✔️ When you **collect a coin**, the **Coins variable** goes up!
✔️ When you **lose a life**, the **Lives variable** goes down!
✔️ When you **win a level**, the **Level variable** increases!

Without variables, games wouldn't know **how many points you’ve scored** or **when you’ve won**. Every time you see numbers changing in a game, **a variable is working behind the scenes!**

## Class 6: Wait Until Loop / Force Sensor

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 6: Wait Until Loop / Force Sensor

The force sensor detects physical force in Newtons (a unit of measurement). It senses in three values:
1. Pressed - force detected above 0 Newtons
2. Hard-Pressed - force detected above 5 Newtons
3. Released - force detected  0 Newtons (no force)

Below are the blue sensor block options we have when using the force sensor. The pointed block is a conditional block that is waiting until the force sensor meet a condition. The rounded block is to read the values of the force sensor.
![code](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/bluetouch.png)One of the most common ways to use the force sensor is with the **"Wait Until"** control block. Think of this block like a stop sign for your program—it pauses execution until a specific condition is met. However, unlike a real stop sign, any code that was already running will **keep going** until the condition is fulfilled. For example, if a **"Start Moving"** block is already active, the robot will continue moving until another **Motor Pair** block tells it to stop.

The "force sensor is pressed" is a conditional block that is waiting until the the condition of being pressed is "True" before it moves to the next line of code.

![code](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/wup.png)
 

Below is an example of how we would use this as a full program. The robot starts and continues moving straight until the force sensor is pressed when it runs into the wall. 

![code](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/wupf.png)

# Class 8: Ultrasonic Sensor
## Class 8: Ultrasonic sensor

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 8: Ultrasonic sensor

![robot](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/ushouse.png)

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 8: Ultrasonic sensor

![robot](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/ushouse.png)

The LEGO SPIKE Prime ultrasonic sensor is like giving your robot a pair of "bat ears" that let it “see” objects using sound waves. It works by emitting a high-pitched sound that’s way above what humans can hear. The sensor sends out this sound and then listens for the echo as it bounces back from an object. By measuring how long it takes for the sound to return, the sensor calculates the distance between the robot and whatever is in front of it. It’s like your robot shouting, "Hello?" and listening to hear how far away the reply comes from. This makes it perfect for navigating, avoiding obstacles, and detecting objects like a super-smart robot explorer!

# Class 9: Passive Attachments Part 2
## Class 8: Passive Attachments - One Way Door

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 8: Passive Attachments - One Way Door

 

The passive attachment we are going to build next is called a one way door (or a one way switch).

![One way door](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R600V2/640/owd.png) The action of this device allows something to pass in but not out.  Watch the video below to see how this works. A great place to use a one way door is when a mission requires something to be pulled.

[Video: One Way Door](https://player.vimeo.com/video/952769680)

[Video: One Way Door](https://player.vimeo.com/video/952769680)


Now that you've seen a demonstration of the one-way door, it's time to build your own! The key to success is ensuring that your pivot connection—the swinging component—is secured with a gray or tan frictionless pin, allowing it to move smoothly. Additionally, you'll need a stopper to prevent it from swinging in the opposite direction.
**Example Photos: **

![One way door](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/paa.png)

![One way door](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/R400V2/pab.png)

# Class 1: Introduction to Self Driving Vehicle
## Class 1 : Introduction to Drive Train

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 1 : Introduction to Drive Train

### **What is a Steering Mechanism?**

A steering mechanism is the system in a car that changes the direction of the front wheels, allowing the vehicle to turn left or right. Without steering, the car would only move in a straight line.

### **Real-Life Example:**

Imagine you're riding a bicycle. When you turn the handlebars, the front wheel turns in the direction you want to go — left or right. A car works similarly, but with two front wheels that must turn together and at the right angle.

metadata.title: Class 1: Introduction to Self Driving Vehicle

page_content:
### **Why Can’t All Wheels Turn?**

Only the front wheels turn because they’re responsible for steering. The rear wheels follow the direction set by the front.

### **Ackermann Steering Geometry**

In most cars, the front wheels don't turn at the same angle. When turning, the inner wheel needs to turn sharper than the outer wheel because it's following a smaller circle.

This is called Ackermann Steering.

#### Visualize it like this:

If your car is turning left:


- 

The left wheel (inside the turn) turns more.


- 

The right wheel (outside the turn) turns a little.



This makes the car turn smoothly without slipping or dragging.

# Class 2 : Introduction to Zebra Bot
## Class 2: Introduction to Microcontrollers

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 2: Introduction to Microcontrollers

Imagine a tiny computer, smaller than your palm, quietly running behind the scenes in many devices you use every day. This small computer, known as a microcontroller, acts as the brain that controls specific tasks within electronics.

Unlike the big computers you use at school or home, a microcontroller is designed to do **one job really well**. It’s found inside devices like TV remotes, video game controllers, microwaves, washing machines, and even in robots!

### **What’s Inside a Microcontroller?**

### **What’s Inside a Microcontroller?**

A microcontroller has several important parts packed into one chip:


- 

**CPU (Central Processing Unit):** This is the “brain” of the microcontroller. It performs all the thinking and calculations.


- 

**Memory:** Just like a notepad, it temporarily stores essential information — like which button you pressed or sensor data it needs to remember.


- 

**Input/Output (I/O) Pins:** These are used to connect the microcontroller to buttons, LEDs, motors, sensors, and displays.


- 

**Timers, Counters, and Communication Ports:** These enable interaction with other devices and the management of time-based actions.



![S3 access](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/T720/Class1/c1.JPG)

### **What Does a Microcontroller Do?**

![S3 access](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/T720/Class1/c1.JPG)

### **What Does a Microcontroller Do?**

The microcontroller waits for input (like pressing a button), processes the input (thinks about what to do), and gives an output (like turning on an LED or starting a motor). It does all of this very quickly and automatically.

### **Real-Life Examples:**


- 

In a TV remote, the microcontroller reads your button press and sends the correct signal to the TV.


- 

In a microwave, it controls the timer, display, and heating based on your selection.


- 

A robot can read sensors and make decisions — like turning left when it detects an obstacle.


 

Microcontrollers may be small, but they’re incredibly powerful — they’re the hidden heroes that bring smart devices to life!

## Class 2: Robot Safety & Care Guidelines

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 2: Robot Safety & Care Guidelines

Robots are amazing — but they’re also delicate! To keep our robot team in top shape, please follow these important rules:


- 

**Always carry your robot with both hands.** It helps prevent accidental drops and damage.


- 

**Never run your robot on a table without side barriers.** Robots can be unpredictable — we don’t want them diving off the edge!


-

- 

**Be gentle when plugging in motors and sensors.** Only handle the plastic connector at the end of the wire. Avoid pulling or yanking the wires.


- 

**Never pull a motor or sensor out by the cord.** Always insert and remove them by gripping the plastic connector. (Watch the demonstration video below for guidance.)


- 

**Avoid swinging motors or sensors by their wires.** Always hold them by their plastic casing to prevent internal damage.


- 

**Keep laptops on proper surfaces.** Never place them on the floor or against the walls of the robot challenge table. They should always rest on a flat desk or table.



By taking good care of your robot and tools, you'll ensure everything works smoothly — and your robot lives to complete another mission!

# Class 3: Elements of Coding
## Class 3: Elements of Coding

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 3: Elements of Coding

Coding is like giving instructions to a robot or computer so it knows exactly what to do. Every program — from a simple blinking light to a self-driving car — is built using a few core building blocks. Once you understand these building blocks, or elements, coding becomes much easier to read, write, and debug.

Coding can be thought of as two parts:


- 

**Algorithm** – the logic or step-by-step plan


- 

**Programming Language** – the grammar/syntax we use to write that logic



Let’s break coding down into six essential elements that form the foundation of every program:

Let’s break coding down into six essential elements that form the foundation of every program:

### 1.  **Data**

Data is the information your program works with — like numbers, text, or sensor readings.

Example:




*int distance = 30;*


### **2. Variables**

Variables are containers that store data. You can change their values as your program runs.

Example:


*`int speed = 50;
`*

*`speed = 70; // update the value`*






### 3.  **Statements**

Statements are single lines of code that tell the program to do something — like a command or instruction.

Example:


*`digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, HIGH);`*


### 4.  **Operators**

Operators are symbols used to perform actions like math or comparisons.

Example:


`int total = 5 + 3;  // '+' is an arithmetic operator`

Example:


`int total = 5 + 3;  // '+' is an arithmetic operator`


### 5.  **Conditions**

Conditions let your program make decisions. They verify the truth of something and then act accordingly.

Example:


*`if (distance < 20) `*

*`{
`*

*`  digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, HIGH);`*

*`}`*


### **6.  Loops**

Loops run code repeatedly until a condition is met — perfect for repeating actions.

Example:


*`for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) `*

*`{
`*

*   `  digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, HIGH);
`*

*`  delay(500);
`*

*`  digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, LOW);
`*

*   `  delay(500);
`*

*`}`*


### **Summary**

*`  delay(500);
`*

*`  digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, LOW);
`*

*   `  delay(500);
`*

*`}`*


### **Summary**

Mastering these six elements will give you the tools to write your programs, troubleshoot problems, and build smarter, more complex projects. Think of them as your coding toolbox — the more comfortable you get with them, the more creative you can be!

## Class 3: Data and Variables

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 3: Data and Variables

### **What is Data?**

**Data** is the foundation of all coding. Every line of code — even something as simple as:


*`Serial.println("Hello, world!");`*


Coding is essentially the process of manipulating data to control how computers or robots act and respond to input.

Coding is essentially the process of manipulating data to control how computers or robots act and respond to input.

There are **three core data types**, and many others are combinations or extensions of these:

#### **1. Boolean**


- 

Simplest data type: only two possible values — `true` or `false`


- 

In electronics, it's often represented as `1` or `0` (HIGH or LOW)


- 

Commonly used in conditions and digital sensor readings




`*bool isObstacle = false;*`


#### **2. Numbers**


- 

Most frequently used data type in coding


- 

Subtypes include:


  - 

`int` – whole numbers (e.g. `*int count = 10;*`)


  - 

`float` – decimal numbers (e.g. `*float temp = 25.6;*`)


  - 

`long` / `double` – for more precision or larger ranges




-

- 

`long` / `double` – for more precision or larger ranges




- 

Critical for calculations, sensor values, timers, and more


 

#### **3. Characters & Strings**


- 

Characters (`char`) represent single letters or symbols


- 

Strings are collections of characters used for displaying text





*`char grade = 'A';
String message = "System Ready";`*

**Note:** Text-based data is less common in basic embedded electronics but important for displays and user interfaces.

 

### **Sensor Data Examples:**



| Sensor Type | Data Type | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Digital Switch | Boolean | `HIGH` (1) or `LOW` (0) |
| Temperature Sensor | Number (`float`) | `23.4°C`, `float temp = analogRead(A0);` |
| LCD Display | Character/String | Showing `"Hello"` |

## **What is a Variable?**

A variable is a named container used to store data in a program. Think of it like a labelled box you can put data into and change whenever you need.

Each variable has:


- 

**A name** – what you call the box


- 

**A type** – the kind of data it stores (int, float, etc.)


- 

**A value** – the actual information stored



### **Example:**


![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/Image3.3.PNG)



- 

`int` → variable type (integer)


- 

`pin` → variable name


- 

`13` → value assigned



You can now use this variable in your code like this:



*`pinMode(pin, OUTPUT);
digitalWrite(pin, HIGH);
`*



The benefit? If you ever want to change the pin number, you only update it once at the top of your code!




### **Why Are Variables Useful?**

### **Why Are Variables Useful?**

Variables are especially powerful when dealing with values that change constantly, like sensor inputs or motor speeds.

For example, to control LED brightness using PWM:


*`int brightness = 128;
`*

*`analogWrite(ledPin, brightness);
`*


You can update the `brightness` variable over time to make the LED fade in and out.

### **In Summary:**


- 

**Data** is the information we use in our code — numbers, text, or true/false


- 

**Variables** are reusable, named containers that hold this data and make our programs dynamic, flexible, and readable



Mastering these concepts is key to writing smarter, more adaptable code!

The SSH1106 OLED display is a small, lightweight screen that shows important information from your robot in real time. This makes your robot not only more interactive but also much easier to test and troubleshoot.

### **What is the SSH1106?**

### **What is the SSH1106?**

The SSH1106 is a controller chip used in 128x64 pixel monochrome OLED displays. It's very similar to the more common SSD1306, but it has slight differences in how it handles memory addressing. The display communicates with your microcontroller over I2C, making it easy to wire and use with just two data pins.

Outputting current robot speed or direction

**Introduction to Using Buttons**

Buttons are one of the simplest and most common inputs you can add to a project. They give users a direct way to send a signal to the microcontroller — for example, “Start now!” or “Change mode.”

**How a Button Works**
A button is essentially a small switch.


- 

When pressed, it **closes** the circuit, allowing current to flow.


- 

When released, it **opens** the circuit, stopping the current.



**Wiring the Button**

Most microcontrollers support a **`INPUT_PULLUP`** mode which uses an internal resistor to keep the input pin at a default HIGH state when the button isn’t pressed. This means:


- 

**Pressed button** → Pin reads **LOW** (connected to ground).


-

- 

**Pressed button** → Pin reads **LOW** (connected to ground).


- 

**Released button** → Pin reads **HIGH** (pulled up by the resistor).

# Class 4: Working with Motors
## Class 4: Introduction to DC Motors

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 4: Introduction to DC Motors

[Video: Video](https://player.vimeo.com/video/1105554404)


A DC motor is a device that transforms electrical energy into motion. It operates on direct current (DC) electricity, converting the power into a rotating movement. When you supply it with electricity, it rotates and performs tasks like spinning a fan or propelling a robot. It's like using particular electricity to make things spin in circles!

![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image8.1.png)

**Where do we use them?**

![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image8.1.png)

**Where do we use them?**

Motors are like helpers that make things move when we use electricity. DC motors, in particular, are handy for various tasks. They're great for jobs like moving things on conveyor belts, spinning turntables, and other situations where we need to control speed and have steady, strong turning power. Imagine using a motor to make a toy train run at just the right speed or a factory conveyor belt move smoothly – DC motors are the go-to choice for these jobs!

**How do they work?**

**How do they work?**

The magic of electromagnetism powers DC motors. When electricity flows through a wire, it creates an invisible force called an electromagnetic field. This field makes the rotor (a part inside the motor) spin, and when it spins, it makes the motor's output shaft turn. It's like a secret force that turns the motor and makes machines move. So, DC motors use electricity and magnets to create motion!

![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image8.2.png)

A DC motor has two essential parts: the stator and the rotor. The stator doesn't move; it stays still. But it does something incredible—it creates a magnetic field. This magnetic field makes the rotor (the part that moves) spin around.

Here's how it works: Imagine a coil of wire with electricity flowing through it. This coil is wrapped around the motor's core. When the electricity flows, it creates an electromagnetic field right in the center of the coil. Then, the motor has some fixed magnets (the stator part). These magnets help focus the magnetic field created by the coil, making it spin. This spinning force is what makes the armature move and the motor work! It's like teamwork between magnets and electricity.

## Class 4:  Introduction to Servo Motor

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 4:  Introduction to Servo Motor

## **Introduction to Servo Motors**

Think of a **servo motor** as a highly precise motor — like a robotic arm that knows exactly where to go and stops at just the right spot. Imagine pointing a laser at a target and hitting it perfectly every time — that’s the level of accuracy we get with a servo motor!

### ![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image8.7.png)

### **Why Are Servo Motors So Accurate?**

What gives the servo motor its precision is a tiny internal "brain" called a feedback circuit. This circuit constantly monitors the motor’s position and speed. If something’s off, it immediately corrects it — just like a coach making sure every move is perfect.

We use servo motors in situations where high accuracy is required, such as:


- 

Rotating a robotic arm to grab an object


- 

Controlling steering in a remote-controlled car


- 

Operating camera gimbals, flight controls, and more



They’re basically the steady hands of the robotics world!

###  **How a Servo Motor Works**


Think of a servo motor as a team of three smart parts working together:


1.

###  **How a Servo Motor Works**


Think of a servo motor as a team of three smart parts working together:


1. 

**Motor** – does the movement


2. 

**Output sensor (potentiometer)** – measures the actual position


3. 

**Feedback system (controller)** – compares the target position with the actual one and corrects it



This teamwork ensures every motion is accurate and well-controlled.
 

#### **Internal Components:**

**![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image8.8.png)**

As shown in the diagram below, a servo motor typically includes:


- 

A **DC motor**


- 

A **potentiometer**


- 

A **control circuit**


- 

A **gear assembly** to reduce speed and increase torque



Here's what happens step-by-step:


- 

You send a **target position** signal to the servo


-

Here's what happens step-by-step:


- 

You send a **target position** signal to the servo


- 

The **potentiometer** detects the current shaft position


- 

The controller compares the actual vs. desired position


- 

Any difference (error) is sent as a signal to correct the motor movement



This **closed-loop feedback system** ensures the output is precise.

Class 6: Coding sensors - Time-of-Flight (TOF)

## TOF Sensor (VL53L0X)

The VL53L0X is a small and powerful Time-of-Flight (TOF) distance sensor. Unlike traditional infrared sensors that detect if something is near, this sensor can accurately measure how far an object is — using light!

![image](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDV/image1.8.png)

### **How Does It Work?**

The VL53L0X works like a super-fast stopwatch:


1. 

It sends out an invisible laser beam (don’t worry, it's safe and low-power).


2. 

The light bounces off an object in front of it.


3.

2. 

The light bounces off an object in front of it.


3. 

The sensor measures how long it takes for the light to return.


4. 

Using that time, it calculates the exact distance to the object with accuracy down to the millimetre level.



This is why it’s called a Time-of-Flight sensor: it’s measuring the “flight time” of the light.

### **Why Is It Useful?**


- 

Precise distance measurement (up to ~2 meters)


- 

Works even in low light


- 

Better accuracy than ultrasonic or basic IR sensors


- 

Super small and easy to mount on a robot



**We often use it for:**


- 

Obstacle detection


- 

Wall-following robots


- 

Autonomous navigation


- 

Maintaining a safe distance


 

**ZebraTOF Library**

- 

Wall-following robots


- 

Autonomous navigation


- 

Maintaining a safe distance


 

**ZebraTOF Library**

The **ZebraTOF **library is designed to work with VL53L0X Time-of-Flight (TOF) sensors using an I2C multiplexer. It makes it easy to:


-  Initialize sensors on specific ports

-  Read accurate distance measurements in millimetres

- Automatically switch I2C ports using a TCA9548A multiplexer

## **Gyro Sensor (MPU6050)**

The MPU6050 is a 6-axis motion sensor that combines:


- 

A 3-axis gyroscope – to measure rotation (how fast or how much the robot is turning),


- 

A 3-axis accelerometer – to measure acceleration and tilt (detects if the robot is going up/down or leaning).

- 

A 3-axis accelerometer – to measure acceleration and tilt (detects if the robot is going up/down or leaning).



This sensor is commonly used in mobile phones, drones, and robotics to detect motion, orientation, and balance.

### **What Does It Do for Your Robot?**

Using the MPU6050, your robot can:


- 

Measure how much it turns (yaw, pitch, roll),


- 

Detect sharp movements or tilts,


- 

Help the robot turn accurately by angle (e.g., turn 90°),


- 

Improve autonomous movement (e.g., making smooth or controlled curves).



### **How It Works in Our System**

In this class, we’re using our custom microcontroller and a custom MPU6050 library built specifically for our setup. This allows us to read directly:


- 

Raw gyro values (degrees per second),

Real-time motion data to make decisions.

## Class 6: I2C - RGB Color Sensor

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 6: I2C - RGB Color Sensor

The TCS34725 is a powerful colour sensor that allows your robot to “see” colours — just like how your eyes can tell the difference between red, blue, green, and everything in between.

It works by detecting Red, Green, and Blue (RGB) values from a surface, which can then be used to identify different colours. This sensor also has a built-in infrared blocking filter and white LED, so it gives consistent readings even under varying light conditions.

![image](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDV/image2.0.jpg)

### **How It Works:**


- 

The sensor shines white light onto a surface using its built-in LED.


- 

It then measures the intensity of Red, Green, and Blue light reflected back from the surface.


- 

These values are combined to detect the actual colour.


- 

The sensor can also detect clear (ambient) light to adjust brightness sensitivity.



###  **Features:**



- 

I²C communication (easy to connect with most microcontrollers)


-

###  **Features:**



- 

I²C communication (easy to connect with most microcontrollers)


- 

Built-in white LED for accurate colour detection


- 

Detects a full range of colours


- 

Can differentiate between different shades of the same colour



### **Applications:**


- 

Line following using colour strips


- 

Colour-based sorting (e.g., red object vs. blue object)


- 

Detecting traffic lights in a simulated environment


- 

Triggering different behaviours based on detected colours (e.g., slow down at red)


 

**ZebraBot Libaray: **

The ZebraColour library is designed to work with TCS34725 RGB colour sensors via an I2C multiplexer. It helps you:


- 
Initialize colour sensors on specific ports



- 
Read RGB, clear light, and lux data.

- 
Initialize colour sensors on specific ports



- 
Read RGB, clear light, and lux data.



- 
Automatically switch I2C ports using a TCA9548A multiplexer.



- Identify basic colours (Yellow, Red, Blue, Green, Black)

*`   ZebraColour colourSensor(1);`* // Replace 1 with 1-6 depending on which port your sensor is connected to.

# Class 7: HuskyLens AI Camera
## Class 7: Introduction to HuskyLens

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 7: Introduction to HuskyLens 

The **HuskyLens** is an easy-to-use **AI vision sensor** that allows robots to *see* and *understand* the world around them. Unlike regular sensors that only measure distance or color, HuskyLens uses a built-in camera and artificial intelligence to recognize **objects, faces, colors, lines, and tags**.

In our self-driving car project, the HuskyLens acts like the **eyes of the robot**, helping it make smarter decisions based on what it sees.

---


## **Why Use HuskyLens in a Self-Driving Car?**

Self-driving vehicles rely heavily on cameras to:


-

---


## **Why Use HuskyLens in a Self-Driving Car?**

Self-driving vehicles rely heavily on cameras to:


- 

Follow lanes


- 

Detect obstacles


- 

Recognize signs or markers


- 

Make navigation decisions



HuskyLens allows students to explore these real-world concepts **without needing advanced AI programming**. The AI models are trained directly on the device, making it perfect for learning and prototyping.

---


## **Key Features of HuskyLens**


- 

**Built-in AI processor** (no external AI computer needed)


- 

**Multiple AI modes**, including:


  - 

Object Tracking


  - 

Object Recognition


  - 

Color Recognition


  - 

Line Tracking


  - 

Tag (AprilTag-like) Recognition




- 

**Onboard screen** to preview what the camera sees


-

Line Tracking


  - 

Tag (AprilTag-like) Recognition




- 

**Onboard screen** to preview what the camera sees


- 

**I2C and UART communication** for easy connection to microcontrollers


- 

Works well with **Arduino-based and custom controllers**



---


## **Communication with the Microcontroller**

In this course, the HuskyLens communicates with the robot controller using **I2C or Serial (UART)** communication.


- 

The camera processes the image internally


- 

It sends **processed results** (such as object position, ID, or size) to the microcontroller


- 

The microcontroller uses this data to control motors and steering



This approach keeps the code simple while still demonstrating powerful AI concepts.

---


## **Using Serial Monitor for Debugging**

---


## **Using Serial Monitor for Debugging**

To better understand how the camera works, we use **sample code** that prints HuskyLens data to the **Serial Monitor / Terminal**.

This allows students to:


- 

See real-time values such as:


  - 

Object ID


  - 

X and Y position


  - 

Width and height of detected objects




- 

Verify that the camera is detecting objects correctly


- 

Debug their logic before connecting the data to motors



Using the serial monitor helps bridge the gap between **what the robot sees** and **how the code reacts**.

---


## **Learning Outcomes**

By working with the HuskyLens camera, students will learn how to:


- 

Use vision sensors in robotics


- 

Understand how AI perception works in autonomous vehicles


-

# Class 8: Introduction to CAD
## Class 8: Introduction to 3D Designing

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 8: Introduction to 3D Designing

3D Design is the process of using software to mathematically represent any 3D Object. Various software and web-based tools and solutions are available for 3D Design. 

![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image5.1.0.png)![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image5.1.1.png)

What is the use of 3D Design?

3D designing is used in multiple sectors of the industry. The most common of them are as follows:


- Architecture Designing

- Automobile Industry

- Visual Effects and animation

- Virtual Reality and Virtual Environment

- Automobile Industry

- Visual Effects and animation

- Virtual Reality and Virtual Environment

- Computer Aided Manufacturing

- Product Designing


Various 3D Design software applications are used for multiple purposes.

In this course, we will learn 3D Design using an online solution, **“TinkerCAD.”**

**TinkerCAD:**

[Video: Video](//player.vimeo.com/video/1110136192)


**Tools in TinkerCAD:**

List of tools and what they do in Tinkercad:

**Viewcube**: The cube at the top left of the software is known as the Viewcube. It helps you rotate the CAD model in all directions and gain different perspectives.

**Home View**: The home view tool is the first of the five icons on the left of the work plane. The home view helps zoom out of the CAD model and gain an overall perspective of the model.

**Fit All In View**: The Fit All In View icon is underneath the Home View button. It helps you Zoom into a selected component of your CAD model and deal with it separately. 

**Zoom In**: The Zoom In button is the icon underneath the Fit All In view. It helps you zoom into the CAD model.

**Zoom Out**: The Zoom Out button is the icon underneath the Zoom In button. It helps you Zoom out of the CAD model. 

**Perspective View/Flat View**: This button is underneath the Zoom Out icon. It helps switch between Flat View and Perspective View in the CAD model.

**Copy**: The Copy Tool is the top left tool in the toolbar above. It helps you copy components from the CAD model. 

**Paste**: The tool beside the Copy Tool is the Paste Tool. It helps you paste the copied component into the workplace.

**Duplicate**: The tool beside Paste is the Duplicate Tool. It automatically copies the component and moves it in a certain way, allowing you to duplicate it multiple times to create a pattern. 

**Delete**: The tool beside Duplicate is the Delete Tool. It helps you delete components you no longer need in your CAD design. 

**Undo and Redo buttons**: The Undo and Redo buttons help you redo previous changes or undo what you have already done in your CAD design.

**Group**: The third tool on the right is the Group Tool. It helps you combine multiple components into one piece. 

**Ungroup**: The Ungroup tool is on the right of the Group Tool. It helps you break up grouped objects into multiple components. 

**Align Tool:** The Align tool aligns components on the x-axis, y-axis, and z-axis, grouping or lining them up into a CAD model.

**Mirror**: The Mirror tool is beside the Align tool. It helps you flip components in the opposite direction, either horizontally or vertically. 

**Import, Export, or Send to: **You can import files into your CAD Model to make assemblies, export the files to perform 3D printing or laser cutting, or send the CAD file to the person you want to send it to.

## Class 8: Design Your Car

<!-- type: lesson -->

Class 8: Design Your Car

This is your first step towards designing your car. When we talk about designing a car by ourselves, there are a few things that we need to take care of. Designing a vehicle does not mean designing a body; what matters the most is the support structure that holds the whole car together.

![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/Image6.0.PNG)

![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/Image6.0.PNG)

That support structure is called the Chassis. The chassis is the main frame that holds everything into it to make a car work. There are other essential parts that we will be designing, but the most important parts are as follows:


1. Chassis

2. Steering Mechanism

3. Motor mounts

4. Wheel mounts

5. Circuit plate


We can not design all these parts one by one or simultaneously. We need to start with one and continue modifying it based on the other parts as we begin designing the chassis. Then, we start working on the steering mechanism and motor mounts and modify the chassis to make it compatible with them. We also need to keep assembling the parts to get an idea of the overall look of the car structure.

## **Design Your Own Chassis Using Tinkercad**

Now it’s time to apply everything you've learned and design your own robot chassis using Tinkercad. This will serve as the foundation of your robot — supporting the motors, wheels, electronics, and the steering system.

### **Base Chassis Design**

Your chassis is like the skeleton of your robot. It's what everything else connects to, so **structural strength and accurate spacing** are key.

Here are some reference images to help you understand the layout and structure:

#### ![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image6.1.png)

#### ![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image6.1.png)

The front of the chassis includes **holes and slots for mounting the steering mechanism**. Make sure these are well-aligned so the steering system can turn smoothly.

#### ![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/Image6.2.png)

Below is an image showing the complete chassis with **dimensions**. Use these as a reference when designing your own version in Tinkercad.


 Tip: Be mindful of measurements! Your motors, wheels, and servo need to fit comfortably.


### ![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image6.3.png)

### **Steering Mechanism Overview**

### ![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image6.3.png)

### **Steering Mechanism Overview**

After the chassis, the steering mechanism is the second most critical part of your robot car. It allows you to control direction and turning with precision.

In this class, you’ll be designing a 2-bar steering system, which is simple and highly effective — perfect for small robotic cars like yours.
 

#### **Types of Steering Mechanisms:**


1. 

**4-Bar (or 2-Bar) Steering System** – Common in RC cars and robotics due to its simplicity.


2. 

**Rack-and-Pinion System** – More complex, used in real cars for smoother control and precision.



### **How the 2-Bar Steering System Works**

**![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image6.6.0.png)**

### **How the 2-Bar Steering System Works**

**![image ](https://dp4rp4tcpuoo1.cloudfront.net/SDC/image6.6.0.png)**

Imagine a small parallelogram that turns the wheels:


- 

The **first bar** is a strong base that supports the wheels and servo. It's mounted to the chassis.


- 

The **second bar** (usually colored pink in diagrams) connects to the **servo motor**.


- 

When the servo turns, it **moves the second bar**, which rotates the wheels to steer the robot.

 ZebraGyro 
 The  ZebraGyro  library  is  designed  for  precise  orientation  tracking  using  the  MPU6050 
 gyroscope  in  DMP  (Digital  Motion  Processor)  mode  .  It  provides  smooth  and  reliable  yaw 
 measurements  and  supports  continuous  angle  tracking  beyond  360°.  The  library  is  compatible 
 with  systems  that  use  a  TCA9548A  I²C  multiplexer  ,  allowing  multiple  gyroscopes  to  be  used  on 
 the same bus. 
 Key Capabilities 
 ●  Uses the MPU6050’s DMP for accurate yaw calculation 
 ●  Tracks  continuous yaw rotation  (not limited to 0–360°) 
 ●  Supports multiple gyros via the TCA9548A I²C multiplexer 
 ●  Provides simple methods for updating, reading, and resetting yaw

 Parameters 
 ●  First parameter: RX pin (ESP32 receives data from HuskyLens)

 isLearned() 
 Checks whether the HuskyLens has learned at least one object/color (depends on selected 
 algorithm mode). 
 Returns 
 ●  true if at least one object is learned 
 ●  false if nothing is learned 
 Example 
 if  (!husky.isLearned()) { 
 Serial.println(  "Please train HuskyLens first!"  ); 
 return  ; 
 } 
 4. 
 isDetected() 
 Checks whether any object is currently detected. 
 Returns

 getID() 
 Returns the learned ID of the first detected object.

 getWidth() 
 Returns width of the first detected object (pixels).

 Multi-Object and Advanced Functions

 getY(int index)

Z-Bot (Marina) Libraries Guide 
 Training HuskyLens 
 Color Recognition Mode 
 1.  Power on HuskyLens 
 2.  Select  Color Recognition  mode 
 3.  Point the camera at the colored object 
 4.  Press the button once (bounding box appears) 
 5.  Press the button again to confirm and assign a tag (ID 1–7) 
 Object Detection Mode 
 1.  Power on HuskyLens 
 2.  Select Object Tracking mode 
 3.  Point the camera at the object 
 4.  Press the button once (bounding box appears) 
 5.  Press the button again to confirm
